green.text: Tell the group one thing you genuinely believe about yourself that most people get wrong.
Ask someone at this table a question you're actually curious about.
Name one person at this table you feel most comfortable around right now.
Tell the group something you've never said out loud before — keep it small.
Everyone: what's one word that describes your mood right now? Go around the table.
Say one true thing about this group that no one has said yet tonight.
Name the person at this table you've been most curious about lately.
Tell the group something specific that happened to you in the last three days. Where, when, who was there.
green.pressureNote: Specificity makes fabrication expensive — the Liar must commit to verifiable details.

green.text: Look at everyone at this table. Who seems most like themselves right now?
green.pressureNote: Primes the Analyst to start building a baseline read early.

yellow.text: Ask anyone at this table a yes/no question. They must answer out loud.
Say two true things and one lie. Don't label them. The group decides.
Point to the person you think is performing right now. Say why.
Accuse someone of avoiding a real answer tonight. They must respond.
Everyone: point to who you trust least at this table right now. No explanation.
Name one moment tonight that felt rehearsed. Who was involved?
Ask the group: 'Who here is the most different in private than in public?'
Tell someone at this table something you've noticed about them that you've never said.
Name the person who would be hardest to catch in a lie. Explain.
Who at this table do you think has been the most honest tonight? Point to them.
Go back to something you said earlier tonight. Say it again — exactly. Word for word.
yellow.pressureNote: Liars rarely commit their lies to precise memory. Repetition exposes inconsistency.

yellow.text: Name one detail from what someone said tonight that didn't quite add up. Be specific.
yellow.pressureNote: Activates the Analyst's investigative instinct and forces them to show their hand.

yellow.text: Has anyone at this table said something tonight that changed how you see someone else? What was it?
yellow.pressureNote: Makes the Manipulator's influence visible — if they've been working the room, this surfaces it.

yellow.text: Everyone: on a scale of 1 to 3, how honest do you think this person has been tonight? Hold up fingers at the same time.
yellow.pressureNote: Simultaneous reveal prevents social anchoring. Direct pressure on whoever holds the card.

yellow.text: Tell the group one thing you're genuinely uncertain about tonight — not about yourself, about someone else.
yellow.pressureNote: Rewards the Loyal for verbalizing honest doubt. Creates contrast with performers.

red.text: Tell the person to your left one thing you actually think about them. Be honest.
Call out one thing you noticed about someone's behavior tonight. Address them directly.
Say something you believe that you think no one else at this table believes.
Two players must agree on one shared truth about the group. State it out loud together.
Name the person you think has been lying tonight. One name. No softening.
Everyone stays silent for 10 seconds. Look at each other. Then the current player reacts.
Tell the group: what's the most dangerous thing you could honestly say right now?
Everyone asks the current player the same question simultaneously: 'What are you hiding?'
The group picks one thing you said tonight and asks you to prove it's true. You have 30 seconds.
red.pressureNote: Verification demand. The Liar must either double down or retreat. Both are tells.

red.text: Make your accusation. Name someone at this table, say exactly what you think they're hiding, and don't qualify it.
red.pressureNote: Forces the Analyst to commit. Vague accusations waste the moment.

red.text: Everyone look at the current player for five seconds. No talking. Then say one word about what you see.
red.pressureNote: Sustained eye contact and group labeling is deeply uncomfortable for anyone performing.

red.text: Say one uncomfortable truth about yourself — not a story, not a performance. One sentence. Now.
red.pressureNote: The Loyal's natural territory. Creates a visible honesty benchmark for the group.

red.text: Who at this table has been trying hardest to control how they're seen? Point to them. They respond.
red.pressureNote: The Manipulator's core behavior named directly.

blue.text: Everyone says one word that describes this group right now. Go around.
Say something genuinely kind about the person who challenged you hardest tonight.
Name one thing you want to take back from tonight. You don't have to explain.
Ask the group: 'Did tonight feel real to you?' Everyone answers yes or no.
Tell the group one moment tonight that surprised you.

black.text: Final statement: complete this sentence — 'I was honest tonight because ___.' One sentence each.
Without speaking, point to the person you think played the best game tonight.
If this group had a secret, what would it be? One answer each.
Everyone: one word for how you feel right now compared to when you started.
Tell the person you trusted least tonight why you feel that way. Directly, to their face.
black.pressureNote: Post-game confrontation that reveals whether suspicions hardened or softened.

black.text: Everyone: did you play as yourself tonight, or as a version of yourself? Just nod or shake your head.
black.pressureNote: Non-verbal response reduces the ability to craft an answer. Gesture is harder to fake.
